content:
AI still makes things up, but the real risk isn’t the technology. It’s how carelessly we deploy it.

4 min read
Just now

--

Press enter or click to view image in full size

Image generated with AIA few years ago, I published an article asking whether AI hallucinations were reason enough to reject AI altogether. My answer then was no. But rereading that piece recently, I winced a little. The numbers I’d relied on were already outdated.
So I went back to the research. The question hasn’t changed. The answer, it turns out, has gotten more interesting.
So How Often Does AI Actually Hallucinate?
The truth is, there is no single hallucination rate. I kept looking for one definite number, and it doesn’t exist. The same model can be reliable on one task and wildly unreliable on another. It all depends on what you ask it to do.
Give a model a source document and ask it to summarize, and modern systems do remarkably well. On Vectara’s HHEM leaderboard (probably the most cited hallucination benchmark in the industry), today’s frontier models slip up on maybe 1–2.5% of summaries, and the best ones sit near or below 1% (Vectara HHEM Leaderboard; Presenc AI, 2026). Not perfect, but still impressive.
Now take away the source document and make the model answer from memory. The picture changes. OpenAI’s own system card admitted that its o3 reasoning model hallucinated on a third of prompts in the PersonQA benchmark — double the rate of its predecessor, o1 model (OpenAI o3/o4-mini System Card, April 2025). It looks like the newer, smarter model did worse.
And in specialized domains? Stanford researchers found that general-purpose LLMs hallucinated on anywhere from 69% to 88% of the time across 200,000 legal questions. (Dahl, Magesh, Suzgun & Ho, “Large Legal Fictions,” Stanford RegLab/HAI, 2024). If a paralegal made things up that often, they wouldn’t last a week.
The Reasoning Paradox
That system card result points to something I find counterintuitive. We all assumed hallucinations were a growing pain. Models get smarter, hallucinations fade. Except that’s not quite what’s happening.
When Vectara toughened its benchmark with longer, messier documents, the kind that actually cross desks in law firms and hospitals, the advanced “reasoning” models blew past 10% hallucination rates and lost to simpler models. The working theory is a bit funny: these models put so much effort into thinking through an answer that they overthink it, drifting away from what the source actually says (Vectara Hallucination Leaderboard, 2025–2026).
Worse, MIT research found that models are about 34% more likely to use confident language — words like “definitely” and “certainly” — precisely when they are hallucinating than when they are correct. Which is why some of their most promising current work focuses on teaching models to say “I’m not sure” (MIT News, 2026).
But Let’s Talk About Human Errors
Before we get too smug about machine mistakes, a reality check. CNN has reported that US emergency rooms produce over 7 million incorrect diagnoses every year. The construction industry loses an estimated $273 billion annually to errors in the US alone — around $1.8 trillion worldwide. Nobody proposes shutting down hospitals or construction sites over this.
The real question was never whether AI makes mistakes. Of course it does. The question is whether humans and AI together can make fewer mistakes than either would alone.
What Actually Works
The past two years of research taught me one lesson: how you deploy AI matters more than which model you pick.
Grounding is the big one. Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) — letting the model work from verified documents instead of its own memory — cuts hallucinations by 75–90% in reported deployments (Axis Intelligence, 2026).
Simply switching on web search helps enormously too, with some benchmarks showing 73–86% reductions on factual questions (Suprmind, 2026).
And there’s a third trick: asking the same question to several independent models and trusting only the answers they agree on. Models are surprisingly good at catching each other’s fabrications.
Can we get to zero? Apparently not. A 2025 mathematical analysis argues that no mitigation can eliminate hallucinations entirely (Karpowicz, arXiv:2506.06382). I find that oddly reassuring. It means the goal was never perfection; it’s risk management.
We don’t demand perfection from doctors or engineers either. We demand review, verification, and accountability. AI deserves the same treatment.
Where Does This Lead Us?
When I wrote the original article, my argument was: yes, AI hallucinates, but humans mess up too, so don’t reject it. I still believe that.
A well-architected AI system is already highly reliable. A carelessly deployed one is risky. The dividing line was never between “AI” and “no AI.” It rather runs between responsible implementation and naive implementation. Ground your AI in verified data, add verification layers, keep humans in the loop where the stakes are high, and you get a tool that complements human judgment instead of replacing it. Reject it outright, and you’re not avoiding errors.
References:

• Vectara Hughes Hallucination Evaluation Model (HHEM) Leaderboard, 2025–2026 updates

• OpenAI, o3 and o4-mini System Card, April 2025 (PersonQA hallucination rates)

• Dahl, Magesh, Suzgun & Ho, “Large Legal Fictions: Profiling Legal Hallucinations in Large Language Models,” Stanford RegLab & HAI, 2024

• News MIT, “Teaching AI Models to Say I’m not Sure,” April 2026

• Karpowicz, M. P., “On the Fundamental Impossibility of Hallucination Control in Large Language Models,” October 2025

• Presenc AI, “AI Hallucination Rate Benchmarks 2026”

• Axis Intelligence, “AI Hallucination Statistics 2026”

• Suprmind, “AI Hallucination Rates & Benchmarks for New AI Models,” July 2026

• CNN — on annual misdiagnoses in US emergency rooms
10. Industry reports on the US and global construction error cost
